Appendix A Additional Details and Theoretical Analysis

A.1 Hadamard Transform

The Hadamard transform is an orthogonal mixing transform with entries of equal magnitude. We use the normalized Walsh-Hadamard matrix HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}}, which satisfies HHad⊤​HHad=IH_{\mathrm{Had}}^{\top}H_{\mathrm{Had}}=I. Its dimension is inferred from the key or value vector being transformed. For power-of-two dimensions, it can be defined recursively by H1=[1]H_{1}=[1] and

H2​m=12​[HmHmHm−Hm].H_{2m}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix}H_{m}&H_{m}\\ H_{m}&-H_{m}\end{bmatrix}.

In low-bit LLM quantization, Hadamard transforms are commonly used to spread outlier energy across channels before rounding [9]. In OSCAR, HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}} is composed after the attention-aware covariance rotation to further smooth the rotated coordinates while keeping the transform fixed and efficient.

A.2 Principal Component Analysis

Given a symmetric positive semidefinite matrix A∈ℝd×dA\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d}, principal component analysis (PCA) diagonalizes it via

A=V​Λ​V⊤,Λ=diag​(λ1,…,λr,…),λ1≥λ2≥⋯≥0.A=V\Lambda V^{\top},\qquad\Lambda=\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{r},\dots),\quad\lambda_{1}\geq\lambda_{2}\geq\cdots\geq 0.

Below we recall the important proposition of PCA  [37].

Proposition 1 (Indication of top-r eigenvectors).

For any 1≤r≤d1\leq r\leq d, let

Vr=[v1,…,vr]∈ℝd×r.V_{r}=[v_{1},\dots,v_{r}]\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times r}.

Then

Vr=argmaxU⊤​U=Ir​tr​(U⊤​A​U),V_{r}=\mathrm{argmax}_{U^{\top}U=I_{r}}\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}AU),

and the optimal value is

maxU⊤​U=Ir⁡tr​(U⊤​A​U)=∑i=1rλi.\max_{U^{\top}U=I_{r}}\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}AU)=\sum_{i=1}^{r}\lambda_{i}.

Note that in our setting, when AA is chosen as a target covariance induced by the attention computation, PCA provides an orthogonal basis aligned with the directions that are most important to preserve under KV compression.

Proof.

Let U∈ℝd×rU\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times r} satisfy U⊤​U=IrU^{\top}U=I_{r}. Since A=V​Λ​V⊤A=V\Lambda V^{\top}, we have

tr​(U⊤​A​U)=tr​(U⊤​V​Λ​V⊤​U).\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}AU)=\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}V\Lambda V^{\top}U).

Define

B=V⊤​U∈ℝd×r.B=V^{\top}U\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times r}.

Because VV is orthogonal,

B⊤​B=U⊤​V​V⊤​U=U⊤​U=Ir.B^{\top}B=U^{\top}VV^{\top}U=U^{\top}U=I_{r}.

Hence the columns of BB are orthonormal. Using cyclicity of trace,

tr​(U⊤​A​U)=tr​(B⊤​Λ​B)=tr​(Λ​B​B⊤).\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}AU)=\mathrm{tr}(B^{\top}\Lambda B)=\mathrm{tr}(\Lambda BB^{\top}).

Write

P=B​B⊤∈ℝd×d.P=BB^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d}.

Then PP is an orthogonal projection matrix of rank rr, so

0≤Pi​i≤1,∑i=1dPi​i=tr​(P)=r.0\leq P_{ii}\leq 1,\qquad\sum_{i=1}^{d}P_{ii}=\mathrm{tr}(P)=r.

Therefore

tr​(U⊤​A​U)=tr​(Λ​P)=∑i=1dλi​Pi​i.\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}AU)=\mathrm{tr}(\Lambda P)=\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_{i}P_{ii}.

Set

wi=Pi​i,i=1,…,d.w_{i}=P_{ii},\qquad i=1,\dots,d.

Then

0≤wi≤1,∑i=1dwi=r,0\leq w_{i}\leq 1,\qquad\sum_{i=1}^{d}w_{i}=r,

and thus

tr​(U⊤​A​U)=∑i=1dλi​wi.\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}AU)=\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_{i}w_{i}.

Since λ1≥λ2≥⋯≥λd\lambda_{1}\geq\lambda_{2}\geq\cdots\geq\lambda_{d}, the quantity ∑i=1dλi​wi\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_{i}w_{i} is maximized by assigning full weight to the largest rr eigenvalues, namely

w1=⋯=wr=1,wr+1=⋯=wd=0.w_{1}=\cdots=w_{r}=1,\qquad w_{r+1}=\cdots=w_{d}=0.

Hence

tr​(U⊤​A​U)≤∑i=1rλi.\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}AU)\leq\sum_{i=1}^{r}\lambda_{i}.

Now choose U=VrU=V_{r}. Then

Vr⊤​A​Vr=Vr⊤​V​Λ​V⊤​Vr=diag​(λ1,…,λr),V_{r}^{\top}AV_{r}=V_{r}^{\top}V\Lambda V^{\top}V_{r}=\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{r}),

so

tr​(Vr⊤​A​Vr)=∑i=1rλi.\mathrm{tr}(V_{r}^{\top}AV_{r})=\sum_{i=1}^{r}\lambda_{i}.

Thus VrV_{r} attains the maximum, and therefore

Vr∈argmaxU⊤​U=Ir​tr​(U⊤​A​U).V_{r}\in\mathrm{argmax}_{U^{\top}U=I_{r}}\mathrm{tr}(U^{\top}AU).

∎

A.3 Target Covariance

Given a matrix X=[x1;…;xN]∈ℝN×dX=[x_{1};\dots;x_{N}]\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} whose rows are feature vectors, its empirical covariance, assuming the rows have been centered, is

CX=1N​X⊤​X=1N​∑i=1Nxi⊤​xi∈ℝd×d.C_{X}=\frac{1}{N}X^{\top}X=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_{i}^{\top}x_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d}.

In this paper, we use the uncentered second moment in the same algebraic form and refer to it as a covariance target. For any direction u∈ℝdu\in\mathbb{R}^{d} with ‖u‖2=1\|u\|_{2}=1, the scalar

u⊤​CX​u=1N​∑i=1N(xi​u)2u^{\top}C_{X}u=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{i}u)^{2}

measures the average squared magnitude of the rows of XX along direction uu. Thus, the eigenvectors of CXC_{X} identify directions where the row activations have large energy, and PCA uses these eigenvectors as a basis aligned with the dominant second-order structure of XX.

In OSCAR, the covariance target is not chosen only from the raw cached tensors. The reason is that the rotation should reduce the downstream attention error, not merely the Euclidean reconstruction error of KK and VV. For keys, the empirical logit distortion satisfies

‖Q​K⊤−Q​K^⊤‖F2=tr​((K−K^)​Q⊤​Q​(K−K^)⊤).\|QK^{\top}-Q\widehat{K}^{\top}\|_{F}^{2}=\mathrm{tr}\!\left((K-\widehat{K})Q^{\top}Q(K-\widehat{K})^{\top}\right).

This identity shows that key errors are weighted by the query covariance Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q. Heuristically, directions with large eigenvalues of Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q are directions in which the queries have large energy, so quantization error in those directions causes larger perturbations to the attention logits. Therefore, we use Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q as the key-side target covariance for determining the raw key rotation.

For values, the downstream output distortion satisfies

‖S​V−S​V^‖F2=tr​((V−V^)⊤​S⊤​S​(V−V^)).\|SV-S\widehat{V}\|_{F}^{2}=\mathrm{tr}\!\left((V-\widehat{V})^{\top}S^{\top}S(V-\widehat{V})\right).

Equivalently, the matrix

V⊤​S⊤​S​V=(S​V)⊤​(S​V)V^{\top}S^{\top}SV=(SV)^{\top}(SV)

is the target covariance of the attention-weighted value activations S​VSV. It measures which value feature directions remain large after aggregation by the attention scores. Hence, V⊤​S⊤​S​VV^{\top}S^{\top}SV is an attention-induced value target covariance, while V⊤​VV^{\top}V is only the raw-cache covariance of values.

Therefore, Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q and V⊤​S⊤​S​VV^{\top}S^{\top}SV are covariance targets in our context: they are second-moment matrices induced by the downstream attention computation. This is why OSCAR estimates rotations from these attention-aware covariance targets rather than from raw-cache targets such as K⊤​KK^{\top}K and V⊤​VV^{\top}V.

A.4 Intuition of the combination RK=UQ​HHad​PKR_{K}=U_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}}P_{K}

The rotation applied to keys before quantization in OSCAR is the product of three orthogonal factors,

RK=UQ​HHad​PK,R_{K}\;=\;U_{Q}\,H_{\mathrm{Had}}\,P_{K},

where UQU_{Q} is the eigenbasis of the key target covariance CQ=Q⊤​QC_{Q}=Q^{\top}Q (Sec. A.3), HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}} is the normalized Walsh–Hadamard matrix (Sec. A.1), and PKP_{K} is a permutation matrix that we describe below. The same construction is used for RVR_{V} with CS=V⊤​S⊤​S​VC_{S}=V^{\top}S^{\top}SV in place of CQC_{Q}. This subsection explains, before proceeding to the formal proof in Sec. A.6, why each factor is needed and why they appear in this order. Each factor addresses a distinct failure mode of per-group low-bit quantization.

Two effects of RKR_{K}.

Recall from Sec. A.3 that the rotated cache K~=K​RK\tilde{K}=KR_{K} is what the quantizer sees, and the resulting attention-logit distortion is

‖Q​K⊤−Q​K^⊤‖F2=tr​(RK⊤​CQ​RK⋅EK),\bigl\|QK^{\top}-Q\widehat{K}^{\top}\bigr\|_{F}^{2}\;=\;\mathrm{tr}\!\left(R_{K}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{K}\cdot E_{K}\right),

where EKE_{K} is the residual covariance of K~−𝒬​(K~)\tilde{K}-\mathcal{Q}(\tilde{K}). The rotation enters in two distinct places:

  1. (i)

    it shapes RK⊤​CQ​RKR_{K}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{K}, the query-importance metric in the rotated coordinates;

  2. (ii)

    it shapes EKE_{K}, because the quantizer’s per-token, per-group min–max scale (Sec. A.5) depends on the channelwise distribution of K~\tilde{K}.

The three factors UQU_{Q}, HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}}, PKP_{K} each address one of these effects in turn.

UQU_{Q}: align channels with query-importance directions.

Eigendecompose

CQ=UQ​ΛQ​UQ⊤,ΛQ=diag​(λ1,…,λd),λ1≥⋯≥λd≥0.C_{Q}\;=\;U_{Q}\Lambda_{Q}U_{Q}^{\top},\qquad\Lambda_{Q}=\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{d}),\qquad\lambda_{1}\geq\cdots\geq\lambda_{d}\geq 0.

Setting RK=UQR_{K}=U_{Q} diagonalizes the importance metric:

RK⊤​CQ​RK=ΛQ.R_{K}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{K}\;=\;\Lambda_{Q}.

Geometrically, the jj-th channel of K~=K​UQ\tilde{K}=KU_{Q} is the projection of each key onto the jj-th query principal direction uju_{j}, and λj=‖Q​uj‖22\lambda_{j}=\|Qu_{j}\|_{2}^{2} measures how much total query energy lies along uju_{j}. Quantization noise on channel jj then propagates to the attention logits with weight λj\lambda_{j}: large-λ\lambda channels are exactly those whose error is most amplified, while small-λ\lambda channels can absorb error almost for free. Sec. A.6 formalizes this via a rearrangement-inequality argument on the surrogate ℒ~K​(Rk)=tr​(Rk⊤​CQ​Rk​EK)\tilde{\mathcal{L}}_{K}(R_{k})=\mathrm{tr}(R_{k}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{k}E_{K}): under the frozen-residual assumption, RK=UQR_{K}=U_{Q} is the orthogonal rotation that minimizes the importance-weighted error.

This is the right first step but it leaves a per-group quantization problem. The eigenspectrum ΛQ\Lambda_{Q} of CQC_{Q} in real LLM activations is sharply anisotropic: a few eigenvalues carry most of tr​(CQ)\mathrm{tr}(C_{Q}). After RK=UQR_{K}=U_{Q}, the rotated key channels inherit this imbalance, with a few channels having much larger variance than the rest. A per-group min–max quantizer with a single scale per group then sets its scale based on the largest channel in each group, and the smaller channels in the same group are quantized at a granularity far coarser than their dynamic range. Hence EKE_{K} has a few very large diagonal entries and many tiny ones, and tr​(EK)\mathrm{tr}(E_{K}) is dominated by the worst groups.

HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}}: equalize on the importance metric, suppress outliers on KK.

The UQU_{Q} step is chosen for the surrogate, which is a Q-side criterion (CQ=Q⊤​QC_{Q}=Q^{\top}Q). The Hadamard composition serves two complementary purposes: on the surrogate side, it equalizes the rotated importance metric across channels; on the actual K distribution, it mixes outlier energy across channels so that the per-group min–max quantizer (Sec. A.5) sees comparable per-channel magnitudes. The first claim is exact; the second is what the standard outlier-suppression literature relies on [9] and only requires that the Hadamard column weights all have equal magnitude.

The exact statement on the importance metric is the following.

Lemma 1 (Diagonal equalization under Hadamard conjugation).

Let Λ∈ℝd×d\Lambda\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d} be diagonal and HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}} the normalized Walsh–Hadamard matrix. Then

(HHad⊤​Λ​HHad)i​i=1d​tr​(Λ),i=1,…,d.\bigl(H_{\mathrm{Had}}^{\top}\Lambda\,H_{\mathrm{Had}}\bigr)_{ii}\;=\;\frac{1}{d}\mathrm{tr}(\Lambda),\qquad i=1,\dots,d.
Proof.

Every entry of HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}} has magnitude 1/d1/\sqrt{d}. Then (HHad⊤​Λ​HHad)i​i=∑jHHad​[j,i]2​Λj​j=1d​∑jΛj​j=1d​tr​(Λ)(H_{\mathrm{Had}}^{\top}\Lambda H_{\mathrm{Had}})_{ii}=\sum_{j}H_{\mathrm{Had}}[j,i]^{2}\,\Lambda_{jj}=\frac{1}{d}\sum_{j}\Lambda_{jj}=\frac{1}{d}\mathrm{tr}(\Lambda), independent of ii. ∎

Applying the lemma to Λ=ΛQ\Lambda=\Lambda_{Q} (which is diagonal because of the UQU_{Q} step) gives

(RK⊤​CQ​RK)i​i=(HHad⊤​ΛQ​HHad)i​i=1d​tr​(CQ),∀i.\bigl(R_{K}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{K}\bigr)_{ii}\;=\;\bigl(H_{\mathrm{Had}}^{\top}\Lambda_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}}\bigr)_{ii}\;=\;\frac{1}{d}\mathrm{tr}(C_{Q}),\qquad\forall\,i.

The peaky eigenspectrum that UQU_{Q} exposed has been replaced by a uniform diagonal: every channel now carries the same importance weight tr​(CQ)/d\mathrm{tr}(C_{Q})/d in the rotated frame.

The K-side effect is heuristic but practically important. After RK=UQ​HHadR_{K}=U_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}}, each row of K~=K​RK\tilde{K}=KR_{K} is a Walsh-signed sum of the principal-direction projections K​UQKU_{Q}, with every entry of weight ±1/d\pm 1/\sqrt{d}. A per-token outlier on any single principal direction is thus redistributed in magnitude 1/d1/\sqrt{d} across all dd channels, shrinking the per-token max by roughly the same factor and bringing the per-group min–max scale into a much tighter range. The frozen residual EKE_{K} becomes much smaller in trace and far more uniform across ii. The price paid is that the importance metric is no longer diagonal: RK⊤​CQ​RK=HHad⊤​ΛQ​HHadR_{K}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{K}=H_{\mathrm{Had}}^{\top}\Lambda_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} has off-diagonals that couple channels. In the regime where per-group quantization noise dominates tr​(EK)\mathrm{tr}(E_{K}), the reduction in tr​(EK)\mathrm{tr}(E_{K}) wins over the loss of importance alignment.

Empirical aside: UQU_{Q} does not accidentally diagonalize ΣK\Sigma_{K}.

One might hope that, because WQW_{Q} and WKW_{K} are trained on the same activation distribution, CQ=Q⊤​QC_{Q}=Q^{\top}Q and ΣK=K⊤​K\Sigma_{K}=K^{\top}K would share an eigenbasis, so that UQU_{Q} would simultaneously serve both the Q-side (importance) and K-side (variance) objectives. Empirically, this is not the case. On Qwen3-8B with 20002000 calibration tokens per layer, the top-8 eigenvector self-alignment ∑k=18|UQQ⊤​UQK|/8\sum_{k=1}^{8}|U_{Q}^{Q\top}U_{Q}^{K}|/8 ranges from 0.050.05 to 0.150.15 across the 36 layers — essentially indistinguishable from the random alignment 1/d≈0.091/\sqrt{d}\approx 0.09 expected at d=128d=128. Rotating ΣK\Sigma_{K} by UQU_{Q} typically reduces the fraction of ΣK\Sigma_{K}’s energy concentrated on the diagonal (e.g., layer 1: 0.90 raw vs. 0.09 after UQU_{Q} rotation; layer 16: 0.16 vs. 0.07). Figure 7 shows a representative layer.

The construction is nevertheless robust because the two factors target different things. Lemma 1 guarantees exact diagonal equalization of the importance metric CQC_{Q} regardless of the eigenbasis of ΣK\Sigma_{K}, since the lemma only relies on ΛQ\Lambda_{Q} being diagonal in the post-UQU_{Q} frame. The K-side outlier-suppression effect of HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}} described above also does not require alignment between UQU_{Q} and the eigenbasis of ΣK\Sigma_{K}: the Walsh-signed mixing shrinks per-token outliers by a factor of 1/d1/\sqrt{d} no matter which orthogonal basis KK was placed in beforehand. The Q-side (UQU_{Q}) and K-side (HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}}) factors thus address genuinely independent objectives and compose without interference, rather than relying on an approximate WQ≈WKW_{Q}\approx W_{K} similarity that does not hold in practice.

PKP_{K}: balance importance across groups.

Hadamard mixing equalizes marginal channel variance, but the per-group quantizer in Sec. A.5 acts on contiguous blocks of GKG_{K} channels at a time. What matters at the group level, beyond marginal variance, is whether each group sees a balanced sample of the underlying importance directions: a group whose GKG_{K} channels are dominated by the same handful of large-λj\lambda_{j} eigenvectors is statistically harder to quantize than a group whose channels span the full range of λj\lambda_{j}.

OSCAR uses a permuted bit-reversal (PBR) ordering. Let σ\sigma be the descending-eigenvalue permutation, so that λσ​(0)≥λσ​(1)≥⋯\lambda_{\sigma(0)}\geq\lambda_{\sigma(1)}\geq\cdots, and let β\beta be the bit-reversal permutation on {0,1,…,d−1}\{0,1,\dots,d-1\}. The permutation matrix PKP_{K} places the eigenvector with the kk-th largest eigenvalue at position β​(k)\beta(k), i.e. (PK):,β​(k)=eσ​(k)(P_{K})_{:,\beta(k)}=e_{\sigma(k)}. For d=128d=128, this yields the placement

top-1↦0,top-2↦64,top-3↦32,top-4↦96,top-5..8↦16,80,48,112,…\text{top-1}\mapsto 0,\quad\text{top-2}\mapsto 64,\quad\text{top-3}\mapsto 32,\quad\text{top-4}\mapsto 96,\quad\text{top-5..8}\mapsto 16,80,48,112,\;\;\dots

The defining property of bit-reversal is that for any power-of-two group size G∣dG\mid d, the top-d/Gd/G eigenvectors land in d/Gd/G distinct groups, exactly one per group; the same recursive balance holds at every coarser binary level. Composed with the Hadamard, this means each per-group quantization block sees one representative from each level of the importance hierarchy, regardless of which group size GKG_{K} is chosen at deployment.

Empirical aside: errors separate after attention.

Figure 8 compares layer-wise errors on Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, measured on raw KV tensors and on the downstream quantities consumed by attention. OSCAR is not designed to minimize plain Euclidean reconstruction error of KK or VV, so its raw KK-MSE and VV-MSE are not always dramatically smaller than rotation-only baselines. The gap becomes much clearer after the attention computation: the errors in Q​K⊤QK^{\top} and S​VSV are substantially lower, and the advantage further propagates to attention-output errors. This is exactly what the covariance targets optimize: the key rotation is aligned with query-side covariance, while the value rotation is aligned with the score-weighted value covariance. The trend is consistent with the UKU_{K} intuition above: aligning channels with query-importance directions need not minimize raw KK error, but it directly lowers the error after multiplication by QQ; analogously, the value target is chosen so that the advantage appears after aggregation by SS.

Worked example: Qwen3-4B-Thinking, layer 10, d=128d=128.

We illustrate the three factors on real activations rather than a stylized spectrum. Calibration sample: layer 10 of Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 (32 query heads, 8 key/value heads, GQA ratio =4=4), T=8000T=8000 tokens of GPQA-diamond context. We use the production importance metric — QQ-side query covariance, averaged within each GQA group of 44 query heads and then across all 88 kv-heads,

CQ=1Hkv​∑h=1Hkv1T⋅g​∑i∈𝒢hQi⊤​Qi,C_{Q}\;=\;\frac{1}{H_{\mathrm{kv}}}\sum_{h=1}^{H_{\mathrm{kv}}}\frac{1}{T\cdot g}\sum_{i\in\mathcal{G}_{h}}Q_{i}^{\top}Q_{i},

The decode-time per-group min–max quantizer uses INT2 with GK=64G_{K}=64 (two groups per head); we deliberately pick GK<dG_{K}<d here so the PBR step has a non-trivial effect (see Step 3 for the GK=dG_{K}=d degenerate case). The KK activations we display are from kv-head 0; the shared rotation RK=UQ​HHad​PKR_{K}=U_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}}P_{K} is the one produced by compute_qqt.

Spectrum at layer 10. Eigendecomposing CQ=UQ​ΛQ​UQ⊤C_{Q}=U_{Q}\Lambda_{Q}U_{Q}^{\top} yields an anisotropic but not pathologically peaky spectrum (sorted descending),

λ1​…​8\displaystyle\lambda_{1\dots 8} ≈(162.5, 85.5, 35.6, 17.4, 11.6, 7.5, 6.1, 5.0),\displaystyle\approx(625,855,356,174,116,75,61,50),
λ121​…​128\displaystyle\lambda_{121\dots 128} ≈(0.24, 0.22, 0.22, 0.20, 0.18, 0.05, 0.027, 0.009).\displaystyle\approx(24,022,022,020,018,005,0027,0009).

The trace is tr​(CQ)≈443\mathrm{tr}(C_{Q})\approx 443. The top eigendirection λ1\lambda_{1} alone accounts for 36.6%36.6\% of the trace and the top eight carry 74.7%74.7\%: λ1/λ¯≈46.9\lambda_{1}/\bar{\lambda}\approx 46.9 where λ¯=tr​(CQ)/d=3.46\bar{\lambda}=\mathrm{tr}(C_{Q})/d=3.46. Importance is concentrated in a low-rank subspace, but not as extremely as one might guess from KK’s own variance — instead, covariance Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q reflects what the attention layer actually queries, which is a softer concentration than where KK’s variance happens to land.

One token, all 128 channels, split into two GK=64G_{K}=64 groups. The INT2 per-group min–max quantizer fits its scale to (maxi∈g⁡K~t,i−mini∈g⁡K~t,i)/3\bigl(\max_{i\in g}\tilde{K}_{t,i}-\min_{i\in g}\tilde{K}_{t,i}\bigr)/3 per token tt per group gg. We visualize what the quantizer sees by printing all 128128 channels for a single typical non-sink token (t=5t=5, kv-head 0), reshaped into an 8×168\times 16 grid (channel jj at grid position (⌊j/16⌋,jmod16)(\lfloor j/16\rfloor,\;j\bmod 16)). The horizontal rule splits the grid into the two GK=64G_{K}=64 groups: rows 0–33 are group 0 (channels 0–6363), rows 44–77 are group 11 (channels 6464–127127). Each matrix is annotated with the per-group max–min.

The raw token in the channel basis:

Kt,⋅=[3.42−0.611.590.27−3.14−0.053.551.092.05−1.051.86−0.03−0.42−0.89−1.542.06−1.74−0.15−0.39−0.89−0.022.550.111.79−0.280.25−1.40−6.09−1.04−0.120.64−0.959.061.170.08−1.490.291.130.470.171.220.5514.19−0.26−0.090.202.390.401.09−0.06−30.620.001.030.57−1.302.47−0.11−0.080.56−0.470.45−0.882.31−1.050.500.440.10−0.35−0.781.46−1.162.561.64−0.090.902.331.092.720.950.201.301.820.541.27−1.050.50−4.471.200.321.36−0.81−0.042.27−0.770.641.270.03−0.200.410.580.791.050.46−0.090.490.29−0.020.201.360.77−1.01−2.331.19−1.77−0.01−0.490.470.910.420.171.38−0.39−0.000.43−0.98−0.500.17−0.50]K_{t,\cdot}\;=\;\left[\begin{array}[]{rrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr}3.42&-0.61&1.59&0.27&-3.14&-0.05&3.55&1.09&2.05&-1.05&1.86&-0.03&-0.42&-0.89&-1.54&2.06\\ -1.74&-0.15&-0.39&-0.89&-0.02&2.55&0.11&1.79&-0.28&0.25&-1.40&-6.09&-1.04&-0.12&0.64&-0.95\\ 9.06&1.17&0.08&-1.49&0.29&1.13&0.47&0.17&1.22&0.55&14.19&-0.26&-0.09&0.20&2.39&0.40\\ 1.09&-0.06&\mathbf{-30.62}&0.00&1.03&0.57&-1.30&2.47&-0.11&-0.08&0.56&-0.47&0.45&-0.88&2.31&-1.05\\ \hline\cr 0.50&0.44&0.10&-0.35&-0.78&1.46&-1.16&2.56&1.64&-0.09&0.90&2.33&1.09&2.72&0.95&0.20\\ 1.30&1.82&0.54&1.27&-1.05&0.50&-4.47&1.20&0.32&1.36&-0.81&-0.04&2.27&-0.77&0.64&1.27\\ 0.03&-0.20&0.41&0.58&0.79&1.05&0.46&-0.09&0.49&0.29&-0.02&0.20&1.36&0.77&-1.01&-2.33\\ 1.19&-1.77&-0.01&-0.49&0.47&0.91&0.42&0.17&1.38&-0.39&-0.00&0.43&-0.98&-0.50&0.17&-0.50\end{array}\right]

group 0: max−min=44.81\max-\min=\boldsymbol{44.81}  | group 11: max−min=7.19\max-\min=\boldsymbol{7.19}.

The two groups are wildly unbalanced: group 0 holds both outliers (−30.62-30.62 at j=50j=50 and 14.1914.19 at j=42j=42); group 11 is benign. Group 0’s INT2 scale must fit a range of 44.8144.81, wasting two bits on a few extreme values while the other ∼60\sim 60 channels of that group quantize at granularity an order of magnitude coarser than their actual dynamic range.

Step 1: K~=K​UQ\tilde{K}=KU_{Q} (eigenbasis rotation). Projecting KK onto the Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q eigendirections gives:

(K​UQ)t,⋅=[0.16−1.751.86−2.421.111.05−0.690.261.11−0.553.39−1.801.781.65−1.49−1.320.05−0.18−2.00−1.273.20−1.470.31−1.070.87−1.54−3.78−0.891.601.030.500.550.490.55−0.90−0.050.170.200.78−1.21−1.06−1.700.630.12−0.282.992.88−0.97−1.422.12−0.94−2.881.30−0.421.370.58−1.46−2.040.28−0.28−2.63−0.20−1.77−0.830.500.12−1.190.81−1.900.40−1.31−0.15−2.311.523.20−0.46−0.77−0.09−1.341.720.40−0.72−0.570.400.84−0.72−1.94−0.111.260.070.09−0.060.87−0.92−0.631.19−2.311.561.901.261.16−0.72−0.56−0.481.210.20−0.26−1.781.71−1.281.071.27−3.320.47−0.43−1.77−1.421.424.99−1.91−0.25−1.020.51−1.65−1.11−8.0715.2829.77](KU_{Q})_{t,\cdot}\;=\;\left[\begin{array}[]{rrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr}0.16&-1.75&1.86&-2.42&1.11&1.05&-0.69&0.26&1.11&-0.55&3.39&-1.80&1.78&1.65&-1.49&-1.32\\ 0.05&-0.18&-2.00&-1.27&3.20&-1.47&0.31&-1.07&0.87&-1.54&-3.78&-0.89&1.60&1.03&0.50&0.55\\ 0.49&0.55&-0.90&-0.05&0.17&0.20&0.78&-1.21&-1.06&-1.70&0.63&0.12&-0.28&2.99&2.88&-0.97\\ -1.42&2.12&-0.94&-2.88&1.30&-0.42&1.37&0.58&-1.46&-2.04&0.28&-0.28&-2.63&-0.20&-1.77&-0.83\\ \hline\cr 0.50&0.12&-1.19&0.81&-1.90&0.40&-1.31&-0.15&-2.31&1.52&3.20&-0.46&-0.77&-0.09&-1.34&1.72\\ 0.40&-0.72&-0.57&0.40&0.84&-0.72&-1.94&-0.11&1.26&0.07&0.09&-0.06&0.87&-0.92&-0.63&1.19\\ -2.31&1.56&1.90&1.26&1.16&-0.72&-0.56&-0.48&1.21&0.20&-0.26&-1.78&1.71&-1.28&1.07&1.27\\ -3.32&0.47&-0.43&-1.77&-1.42&1.42&4.99&-1.91&-0.25&-1.02&0.51&-1.65&-1.11&-8.07&15.28&\mathbf{29.77}\end{array}\right]

group 0: max−min=7.16\max-\min=\boldsymbol{7.16}  | group 11: max−min=37.85\max-\min=\boldsymbol{37.85}.

The raw outlier hasn’t been destroyed; it has been redistributed into the Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q eigenbasis. Because UQU_{Q} is built from Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q, not K⊤​KK^{\top}K, the K-spike at j=50j=50 does not project onto a single UQU_{Q} direction — it spreads across the lower-eigenvalue tail with its largest projection landing in the bottom-rank eigenchannels (the matrix’s bottom-right entries: 29.77,15.28,−8.07,…29.77,15.28,-8.07,\ldots). The diagonal of UQ⊤​CQ​UQU_{Q}^{\top}C_{Q}U_{Q} is exactly ΛQ\Lambda_{Q}, so the per-channel importance is now exactly the eigenvalue, but the K-variance has been pushed into low-importance channels with no improvement in per-group dynamic range — in fact the imbalance between groups has flipped and grown: group 11’s range jumps from 7.197.19 (raw) to 37.8537.85, while group 0’s drops from 44.8144.81 to 7.167.16. Per-group INT2 is now harder, not easier.

Step 2: K~=K​UQ​HHad\tilde{K}=KU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} (Walsh-Hadamard mixing). Multiplying by the orthonormal Walsh-Hadamard HHad∈ℝ128×128H_{\mathrm{Had}}\in\mathbb{R}^{128\times 128} rewrites each output channel as a ±1\pm 1-weighted sum of all 128128 eigenchannels (each scaled by 1/1281/\sqrt{128}). The single-channel spike of 29.7729.77 is distributed across all 128128 outputs with mixed signs:

(K​UQ​HHad)t,⋅=[2.241.08−3.730.26−5.760.924.08−3.39−3.720.295.55−2.673.19−3.70−4.161.80−0.870.644.12−3.185.121.16−6.790.971.91−2.08−4.914.21−1.671.974.47−0.63−2.860.666.731.834.371.49−5.770.721.52−0.05−4.943.97−3.092.284.34−0.371.90−1.30−6.06−1.90−2.583.274.18−3.10−2.310.885.04−2.61−0.07−1.10−2.174.09−3.712.316.89−1.791.12−0.12−5.613.444.100.85−3.943.17−3.510.627.03−5.154.111.23−4.840.19−3.602.163.36−0.01−4.310.173.97−2.022.49−0.04−4.112.573.722.20−3.511.67−5.30−0.014.53−2.49−3.300.322.06−4.403.14−1.10−6.541.40−2.851.725.21−0.324.971.00−5.68−1.243.87−1.45−5.401.67−4.301.203.73−3.39](KU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}})_{t,\cdot}\;=\;\left[\begin{array}[]{rrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr}2.24&1.08&-3.73&0.26&-5.76&0.92&4.08&-3.39&-3.72&0.29&5.55&-2.67&3.19&-3.70&-4.16&1.80\\ -0.87&0.64&4.12&-3.18&5.12&1.16&-6.79&0.97&1.91&-2.08&-4.91&4.21&-1.67&1.97&4.47&-0.63\\ -2.86&0.66&6.73&1.83&4.37&1.49&-5.77&0.72&1.52&-0.05&-4.94&3.97&-3.09&2.28&4.34&-0.37\\ 1.90&-1.30&-6.06&-1.90&-2.58&3.27&4.18&-3.10&-2.31&0.88&5.04&-2.61&-0.07&-1.10&-2.17&4.09\\ \hline\cr-3.71&2.31&6.89&-1.79&1.12&-0.12&-5.61&3.44&4.10&0.85&-3.94&3.17&-3.51&0.62&\mathbf{7.03}&-5.15\\ 4.11&1.23&-4.84&0.19&-3.60&2.16&3.36&-0.01&-4.31&0.17&3.97&-2.02&2.49&-0.04&-4.11&2.57\\ 3.72&2.20&-3.51&1.67&-5.30&-0.01&4.53&-2.49&-3.30&0.32&2.06&-4.40&3.14&-1.10&\mathbf{-6.54}&1.40\\ -2.85&1.72&5.21&-0.32&4.97&1.00&-5.68&-1.24&3.87&-1.45&-5.40&1.67&-4.30&1.20&3.73&-3.39\end{array}\right]

group 0: max−min=13.52\max-\min=\boldsymbol{13.52}  | group 11: max−min=13.58\max-\min=\boldsymbol{13.58}.

Every one of the 128128 entries now sits in a tight band [−6.79, 7.03][-6.79,\,7.03], the two groups are within 0.5%0.5\% of each other in dynamic range, and the absolute range collapses from 37.8537.85 (Step 1) to 13.5813.58 — a 2.8×2.8{\times} reduction. The importance metric also equalizes exactly: Lemma 1 applied to ΛQ\Lambda_{Q} predicts every diagonal entry of HHad⊤​ΛQ​HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}}^{\top}\Lambda_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} collapses to the constant tr​(ΛQ)/d=443/128≈3.46\mathrm{tr}(\Lambda_{Q})/d=443/128\approx 3.46, regardless of how peaky ΛQ\Lambda_{Q} was. Table 10 confirms maxi(RK⊤CQRK)i​i/meani(⋅)=1.00\max_{i}(R_{K}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{K})_{ii}/\mathrm{mean}_{i}(\cdot)=1.00 exactly: the 46.9×46.9{\times} peak in the Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q importance is compressed to 1.00×1.00{\times}. This is the key invariant Step 2 produces; the per-group dynamic range alone is not yet better than pure Hadamard (compare to Step 5 below).

Step 3: K~=K​UQ​HHad​PK\tilde{K}=KU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}}P_{K} (permuted bit-reversal). PKP_{K} is a permutation matrix that reorders the 128128 output channels. After UQ​HHadU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} each Walsh output is a ±1\pm 1-weighted sum of all eigenchannels, but the variances of those sums are not identical (driven by which Walsh row a high-eigenvalue eigvec activates most strongly in). PBR places the kk-th largest eigenvector at Walsh output index β​(k)\beta(k) (the bit-reversal of kk). At GK=64G_{K}=64 this guarantees the top-22 eigenvectors split into the two groups (one each), the top-44 distribute one-per-quartile, and so on for any power-of-two grouping, so no single group inherits a disproportionate share of the high-energy eigenchannels.

(K​UQ​HHad​PK)t,⋅=[2.24−3.71−2.863.72−0.874.111.90−2.85−3.724.101.52−3.301.91−4.31−2.313.87−5.761.124.37−5.305.12−3.60−2.584.973.19−3.51−3.093.14−1.672.49−0.07−4.30−3.736.896.73−3.514.12−4.84−6.065.215.55−3.94−4.942.06−4.913.975.04−5.404.08−5.61−5.774.53−6.793.364.18−5.68−4.167.034.34−6.544.47−4.11−2.173.731.082.310.662.200.641.23−1.301.720.290.85−0.050.32−2.080.170.88−1.450.92−0.121.49−0.011.162.163.271.00−3.700.622.28−1.101.97−0.04−1.101.200.26−1.791.831.67−3.180.19−1.90−0.32−2.673.173.97−4.404.21−2.02−2.611.67−3.393.440.72−2.490.97−0.01−3.10−1.241.80−5.15−0.371.40−0.632.574.09−3.39](KU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}}P_{K})_{t,\cdot}\;=\;\left[\begin{array}[]{rrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr}2.24&-3.71&-2.86&3.72&-0.87&4.11&1.90&-2.85&-3.72&4.10&1.52&-3.30&1.91&-4.31&-2.31&3.87\\ -5.76&1.12&4.37&-5.30&5.12&-3.60&-2.58&4.97&3.19&-3.51&-3.09&3.14&-1.67&2.49&-0.07&-4.30\\ -3.73&6.89&6.73&-3.51&4.12&-4.84&-6.06&5.21&5.55&-3.94&-4.94&2.06&-4.91&3.97&5.04&-5.40\\ 4.08&-5.61&-5.77&4.53&-6.79&3.36&4.18&-5.68&-4.16&\mathbf{7.03}&4.34&\mathbf{-6.54}&4.47&-4.11&-2.17&3.73\\ \hline\cr 1.08&2.31&0.66&2.20&0.64&1.23&-1.30&1.72&0.29&0.85&-0.05&0.32&-2.08&0.17&0.88&-1.45\\ 0.92&-0.12&1.49&-0.01&1.16&2.16&3.27&1.00&-3.70&0.62&2.28&-1.10&1.97&-0.04&-1.10&1.20\\ 0.26&-1.79&1.83&1.67&-3.18&0.19&-1.90&-0.32&-2.67&3.17&3.97&-4.40&4.21&-2.02&-2.61&1.67\\ -3.39&3.44&0.72&-2.49&0.97&-0.01&-3.10&-1.24&1.80&-5.15&-0.37&1.40&-0.63&2.57&4.09&-3.39\end{array}\right]

group 0: max−min=13.82\max-\min=\boldsymbol{13.82}  | group 11: max−min=9.36\max-\min=\boldsymbol{9.36}.

The set of 128128 values is identical to Step 2 — only column-index positions are shuffled — but PKP_{K} has gathered most of the high-magnitude entries ({7.03,−6.54,−6.79,−6.06,6.89,6.73,…}\{7.03,-6.54,-6.79,-6.06,6.89,6.73,\ldots\}) into group 0 (rows 0–33) and left group 11 (rows 44–77) with a much tighter [−5.15,4.21][-5.15,4.21] range. On this particular token PBR has increased one group’s dynamic range slightly (13.52→13.8213.52\to 13.82) while shrinking the other’s (13.58→9.3613.58\to 9.36); the residual on the tighter group drops as (9.36/13.58)2≈0.48(9.36/13.58)^{2}\approx 0.48 while the looser group is essentially unchanged. Averaged over all T=8000T=8000 tokens, this gathering effect reduces the mean per-group max–min from 13.4013.40 (UQ​HHadU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}}) to 11.5811.58 (OSCAR), translating to a 1.23×1.23{\times} reduction in tr​(EK)\mathrm{tr}(E_{K}) (208→169208\to 169). At this layer, PBR carries most of OSCAR’s quantization-residual improvement over UQ​HHadU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} alone; the role of UQ​HHadU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} is to make Lemma 1 fire so that the importance metric is equalized exactly (max/mean=1.00\max/\mathrm{mean}=1.00).

Degenerate case (GK=dG_{K}=d). pure Hadamard, QuaRot-style. A natural simpler alternative is to drop UQU_{Q} entirely and rotate by HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}} alone:

(K​HHad)t,⋅=[2.20−0.203.442.25−1.332.333.832.71−2.34−5.964.205.20−3.46−0.774.424.487.155.23−4.17−3.545.855.28−3.61−1.411.053.94−1.22−2.851.481.032.05−0.461.02−1.19−2.61−2.010.933.00−1.01−3.193.092.20−5.85−5.584.903.06−4.39−3.55−3.07−3.650.484.83−3.59−2.923.385.09−2.81−0.891.220.46−3.49−2.212.880.84−1.531.030.940.19−2.300.205.161.89−1.37−3.524.744.87−3.51−4.254.145.805.485.35−4.44−1.887.125.46−1.44−2.711.373.770.45−2.593.212.03−0.670.81−1.552.51−2.80−3.340.291.81−2.03−0.885.684.70−6.89−5.691.381.07−4.79−2.27−3.94−4.432.415.23−2.12−5.192.622.32−1.78−2.210.400.43−0.79−1.481.862.20](KH_{\mathrm{Had}})_{t,\cdot}\;=\;\left[\begin{array}[]{rrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr}2.20&-0.20&3.44&2.25&-1.33&2.33&3.83&2.71&-2.34&-5.96&4.20&5.20&-3.46&-0.77&4.42&4.48\\ \mathbf{7.15}&5.23&-4.17&-3.54&5.85&5.28&-3.61&-1.41&1.05&3.94&-1.22&-2.85&1.48&1.03&2.05&-0.46\\ 1.02&-1.19&-2.61&-2.01&0.93&3.00&-1.01&-3.19&3.09&2.20&-5.85&-5.58&4.90&3.06&-4.39&-3.55\\ -3.07&-3.65&0.48&4.83&-3.59&-2.92&3.38&5.09&-2.81&-0.89&1.22&0.46&-3.49&-2.21&2.88&0.84\\ \hline\cr-1.53&1.03&0.94&0.19&-2.30&0.20&5.16&1.89&-1.37&-3.52&4.74&4.87&-3.51&-4.25&4.14&5.80\\ 5.48&5.35&-4.44&-1.88&7.12&5.46&-1.44&-2.71&1.37&3.77&0.45&-2.59&3.21&2.03&-0.67&0.81\\ -1.55&2.51&-2.80&-3.34&0.29&1.81&-2.03&-0.88&5.68&4.70&\mathbf{-6.89}&-5.69&1.38&1.07&-4.79&-2.27\\ -3.94&-4.43&2.41&5.23&-2.12&-5.19&2.62&2.32&-1.78&-2.21&0.40&0.43&-0.79&-1.48&1.86&2.20\end{array}\right]

group 0: max−min=13.12\max-\min=\boldsymbol{13.12}  | group 11: max−min=14.01\max-\min=\boldsymbol{14.01}.

Hadamard mixing on its own balances the two groups (13.12≈14.0113.12\approx 14.01) and on this token gives a per-group dynamic range comparable to UQ​HHadU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} (Step 2). The crucial structural difference is in the importance metric: CQC_{Q} is not diagonal in the channel basis, so Lemma 1 does not apply to HHad⊤​CQ​HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}}^{\top}C_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}}. We measure max/mean=1.72\max/\mathrm{mean}=1.72 rather than 1.001.00 — the importance floor is not equalized.

The two “what the quantizer sees” columns make the OSCAR advantage on quantization residual concrete. Pure Hadamard cuts the per-group max–min from 25.725.7 (raw) to 13.313.3 (1.9×1.9{\times}); OSCAR cuts it further to 11.611.6 (2.2×2.2{\times} over raw). Since INT2 residual scales as the square of dynamic range, the tr​(EK)\mathrm{tr}(E_{K}) ratios should be (25.7/11.6)2≈4.9(25.7/11.6)^{2}\approx 4.9 and (13.3/11.6)2≈1.3(13.3/11.6)^{2}\approx 1.3, matching empirical 233/169=1.4233/169=1.4 and 206/169=1.2206/169=1.2 (the empirical ratios are smaller than the squared dynamic range because the per-token correlation structure of the residual matters; the relation is only proportional, not exact). PBR alone contributes the bulk of OSCAR’s advantage over UQ​HHadU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} at this layer (tr​(EK)\mathrm{tr}(E_{K}): 208→169208\to 169, 1.23×1.23{\times}). The importance column tells the orthogonal story: only the UQ​HHadU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} and OSCAR rows achieve max/mean=1.00\max/\mathrm{mean}=1.00, the exact diagonal equalization predicted by Lemma 1; pure Hadamard alone leaves it at 1.721.72, and raw / UQU_{Q} alone leave it at 4343–4747.

Summary.

The three factors are not interchangeable, and each provides a guarantee the others cannot:

  • •

    UQU_{Q} exposes the importance spectrum by diagonalizing CQC_{Q}, so per-channel importance becomes exactly λj\lambda_{j}. By itself it makes per-group dynamic range worse on real activations because the KK-variance gets pushed into low-importance directions, not flattened.

  • •

    HHadH_{\mathrm{Had}}, composed with UQU_{Q}, equalizes the importance-metric diagonal to a constant tr​(CQ)/d\mathrm{tr}(C_{Q})/d via Lemma 1 (max/mean=1.00\max/\mathrm{mean}=1.00 exactly). Without the UQU_{Q} pre-step (pure Hadamard alone), the importance metric is not equalized (max/mean=1.72\max/\mathrm{mean}=1.72 here), so quantization error in any single rotated channel can land disproportionately on a high-importance direction in CQC_{Q} depending on the layer/token distribution.

  • •

    PKP_{K} ensures that for any power-of-two group size GK<dG_{K}<d, the importance hierarchy is recursively balanced across groups. This is where most of OSCAR’s empirical tr​(EK)\mathrm{tr}(E_{K}) reduction over UQ​HHadU_{Q}H_{\mathrm{Had}} comes from on real activations. When GK=dG_{K}=d it is a column permutation within a single group and has no effect; the PBR step is strictly beneficial only for finer grouping.

The same three-factor construction defines RV=US​HHad​PVR_{V}=U_{S}H_{\mathrm{Had}}P_{V} on the value side, with CS=V⊤​S⊤​S​VC_{S}=V^{\top}S^{\top}SV (the attention-score-weighted value covariance) in place of CQC_{Q}.

A.5 Scale Determination and Per-token Clipping Details

Quantization is applied after the composed rotations in Eq. (3). At decoding step tt, the key and value rows are first mapped into the rotated coordinates

k~t=kt​RK,v~t=vt​RV.\widetilde{k}_{t}=k_{t}R_{K},\qquad\widetilde{v}_{t}=v_{t}R_{V}.

Before quantization, the implementation applies token-wise percentile clipping separately to keys and values. Given clip ratios ρK,ρV∈(0,1]\rho_{K},\rho_{V}\in(0,1], define

τt(K)=quantileρK⁡({|k~t,c|}c=1d),τt(V)=quantileρV⁡({|v~t,c|}c=1d).\tau_{t}^{(K)}=\operatorname{quantile}_{\rho_{K}}\Bigl(\{|\widetilde{k}_{t,c}|\}_{c=1}^{d}\Bigr),\qquad\tau_{t}^{(V)}=\operatorname{quantile}_{\rho_{V}}\Bigl(\{|\widetilde{v}_{t,c}|\}_{c=1}^{d}\Bigr).

The clipped rows are

k~tclip=clip⁡(k~t,−τt(K),τt(K)),v~tclip=clip⁡(v~t,−τt(V),τt(V)).\widetilde{k}_{t}^{\mathrm{clip}}=\operatorname{clip}\!\left(\widetilde{k}_{t},-\tau_{t}^{(K)},\tau_{t}^{(K)}\right),\qquad\widetilde{v}_{t}^{\mathrm{clip}}=\operatorname{clip}\!\left(\widetilde{v}_{t},-\tau_{t}^{(V)},\tau_{t}^{(V)}\right).

In the current implementation, the default clip ratios are supplied as fixed runtime/calibration hyperparameters, with typical values ρK=0.96\rho_{K}=0.96 and ρV=0.92\rho_{V}=0.92.

For keys, the default implementation uses asymmetric INT2 quantization, optionally with groups along the channel dimension. Let b=2b=2 and qmax=2b−1=3q_{\max}=2^{b}-1=3. For a group gg of a clipped rotated key row, write

k~t,gclip∈ℝ1×GK,\widetilde{k}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times G_{K}},

where GKG_{K} is the key quantization group size. The implementation computes a dynamic min–max scale and zero point for each token and group:

at,g(K)=minc⁡k~t,g,cclip,bt,g(K)=maxc⁡k~t,g,cclip,a_{t,g}^{(K)}=\min_{c}\widetilde{k}_{t,g,c}^{\mathrm{clip}},\qquad b_{t,g}^{(K)}=\max_{c}\widetilde{k}_{t,g,c}^{\mathrm{clip}},
st,g(K)=bt,g(K)−at,g(K)qmax,zt,g(K)=−at,g(K)st,g(K).s_{t,g}^{(K)}=\frac{b_{t,g}^{(K)}-a_{t,g}^{(K)}}{q_{\max}},\qquad z_{t,g}^{(K)}=-\frac{a_{t,g}^{(K)}}{s_{t,g}^{(K)}}.

The quantized and dequantized key group is then

𝒬+(k~t,gclip)=clip(⌊k~t,gclipst,g(K)+zt,g(K)⌉,0,qmax),\mathcal{Q}^{+}\!\left(\widetilde{k}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right)=\operatorname{clip}\!\left(\left\lfloor\frac{\widetilde{k}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}}{s_{t,g}^{(K)}}+z_{t,g}^{(K)}\right\rceil,0,q_{\max}\right),

and

𝒬​(k~t,gclip)=𝒬−​(𝒬+​(k~t,gclip))=st,g(K)​(𝒬+​(k~t,gclip)−zt,g(K)).\mathcal{Q}\!\left(\widetilde{k}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right)=\mathcal{Q}^{-}\!\left(\mathcal{Q}^{+}\!\left(\widetilde{k}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right)\right)=s_{t,g}^{(K)}\left(\mathcal{Q}^{+}\!\left(\widetilde{k}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right)-z_{t,g}^{(K)}\right).

Thus, unlike a fixed Frobenius-norm scale, the key scale is computed dynamically from the clipped rotated key row, independently for each token and each group. In our default configuration, GK=64G_{K}=64.

Values use the same affine asymmetric INT2 quantization rule. For a group gg of a clipped rotated value row, write

v~t,gclip∈ℝ1×GV,\widetilde{v}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times G_{V}},

where GVG_{V} is the value quantization group size. The implementation computes

at,g(V)=minc⁡v~t,g,cclip,bt,g(V)=maxc⁡v~t,g,cclip,a_{t,g}^{(V)}=\min_{c}\widetilde{v}_{t,g,c}^{\mathrm{clip}},\qquad b_{t,g}^{(V)}=\max_{c}\widetilde{v}_{t,g,c}^{\mathrm{clip}},

and

st,g(V)=bt,g(V)−at,g(V)qmax,zt,g(V)=−at,g(V)st,g(V).s_{t,g}^{(V)}=\frac{b_{t,g}^{(V)}-a_{t,g}^{(V)}}{q_{\max}},\qquad z_{t,g}^{(V)}=-\frac{a_{t,g}^{(V)}}{s_{t,g}^{(V)}}.

The quantized and dequantized value group is

𝒬+(v~t,gclip):=clip(⌊v~t,gclipst,g(V)+zt,g(V)⌉,0,qmax),\mathcal{Q}^{+}\!\left(\widetilde{v}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right):=\operatorname{clip}\!\left(\left\lfloor\frac{\widetilde{v}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}}{s_{t,g}^{(V)}}+z_{t,g}^{(V)}\right\rceil,0,q_{\max}\right),

and

𝒬​(v~t,gclip):=st,g(V)​(𝒬+​(v~t,gclip)−zt,g(V)).\mathcal{Q}\!\left(\widetilde{v}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right):=s_{t,g}^{(V)}\left(\mathcal{Q}^{+}\!\left(\widetilde{v}_{t,g}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right)-z_{t,g}^{(V)}\right).

Finally, after quantization–dequantization in the rotated coordinates, the approximations are mapped back to the original coordinate system:

k^t=𝒬​(k~tclip)​RK⊤,v^t=𝒬​(v~tclip)​RV⊤.\widehat{k}_{t}=\mathcal{Q}\!\left(\widetilde{k}_{t}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right)R_{K}^{\top},\qquad\widehat{v}_{t}=\mathcal{Q}\!\left(\widetilde{v}_{t}^{\mathrm{clip}}\right)R_{V}^{\top}.

A.6 Proof of Theorem. 1

We prove the key-side statement first. The row-vector rotation convention gives the frozen-error surrogate

ℒ~K​(Rk)=tr​(Rk⊤​CQ​Rk​EK),Rk⊤​Rk=Id,\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{K}(R_{k})=\mathrm{tr}\!\left(R_{k}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{k}E_{K}\right),\qquad R_{k}^{\top}R_{k}=I_{d},

where

CQ=UQ​ΛQ​UQ⊤,ΛQ=diag​(λ1,…,λd),λ1≥⋯≥λd≥0,C_{Q}=U_{Q}\Lambda_{Q}U_{Q}^{\top},\qquad\Lambda_{Q}=\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{d}),\qquad\lambda_{1}\geq\cdots\geq\lambda_{d}\geq 0,

and

EK=diag​(μ1,…,μd),μ1≤⋯≤μd.E_{K}=\mathrm{diag}(\mu_{1},\dots,\mu_{d}),\qquad\mu_{1}\leq\cdots\leq\mu_{d}.

Here EKE_{K} is treated as a fixed frozen residual covariance matrix.

Let

Rk=UQ​Z,Z⊤​Z=Id.R_{k}=U_{Q}Z,\qquad Z^{\top}Z=I_{d}.

Substituting into the objective gives

ℒ~K​(Rk)\displaystyle\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{K}(R_{k}) =tr​(Z⊤​UQ⊤​CQ​UQ​Z​EK)\displaystyle=\mathrm{tr}\!\left(Z^{\top}U_{Q}^{\top}C_{Q}U_{Q}ZE_{K}\right)
=tr​(Z⊤​ΛQ​Z​EK).\displaystyle=\mathrm{tr}\!\left(Z^{\top}\Lambda_{Q}ZE_{K}\right).

Writing Z=(zi​j)Z=(z_{ij}), we have

(Z⊤​ΛQ​Z)i​i=∑j=1dλj​zj​i2.\bigl(Z^{\top}\Lambda_{Q}Z\bigr)_{ii}=\sum_{j=1}^{d}\lambda_{j}z_{ji}^{2}.

Therefore,

ℒ~K​(Rk)=∑i=1d∑j=1dμi​λj​zj​i2.\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{K}(R_{k})=\sum_{i=1}^{d}\sum_{j=1}^{d}\mu_{i}\lambda_{j}z_{ji}^{2}. (4)

Since ZZ is orthogonal, the matrix W=(wj​i)W=(w_{ji}) with wj​i=zj​i2w_{ji}=z_{ji}^{2} is doubly stochastic. Hence Eq. (4) is a linear function over the Birkhoff polytope, and its minimum is attained at a permutation matrix.

Thus,

minRk⊤​Rk=Id⁡ℒ~K​(Rk)=minΠ∈𝒫d⁡tr​(Π⊤​ΛQ​Π​EK),\min_{R_{k}^{\top}R_{k}=I_{d}}\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{K}(R_{k})=\min_{\Pi\in\mathcal{P}_{d}}\mathrm{tr}\!\left(\Pi^{\top}\Lambda_{Q}\Pi E_{K}\right),

where 𝒫d\mathcal{P}_{d} denotes the set of d×dd\times d permutation matrices. By the rearrangement inequality, since

λ1≥⋯≥λdandμ1≤⋯≤μd,\lambda_{1}\geq\cdots\geq\lambda_{d}\qquad\text{and}\qquad\mu_{1}\leq\cdots\leq\mu_{d},

the minimum is achieved by pairing the largest λj\lambda_{j} with the smallest μi\mu_{i}. This is achieved by the identity permutation. Therefore, one minimizer is

Z=Id,Z=I_{d},

and hence

Rk=UQ.R_{k}=U_{Q}.

The value-side proof is identical. The value surrogate is

ℒ~V​(Rv)=tr​(Rv⊤​CS​Rv​EV),Rv⊤​Rv=Id,\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{V}(R_{v})=\mathrm{tr}\!\left(R_{v}^{\top}C_{S}R_{v}E_{V}\right),\qquad R_{v}^{\top}R_{v}=I_{d},

where

CS=US​ΛS​US⊤,ΛS=diag​(ν1,…,νd),ν1≥⋯≥νd≥0,C_{S}=U_{S}\Lambda_{S}U_{S}^{\top},\qquad\Lambda_{S}=\mathrm{diag}(\nu_{1},\dots,\nu_{d}),\qquad\nu_{1}\geq\cdots\geq\nu_{d}\geq 0,

and

EV=diag​(η1,…,ηd),η1≤⋯≤ηd.E_{V}=\mathrm{diag}(\eta_{1},\dots,\eta_{d}),\qquad\eta_{1}\leq\cdots\leq\eta_{d}.

Again, EVE_{V} is treated as a fixed frozen residual covariance matrix.

Let

Rv=US​ZV,ZV⊤​ZV=Id.R_{v}=U_{S}Z_{V},\qquad Z_{V}^{\top}Z_{V}=I_{d}.

Then

ℒ~V​(Rv)\displaystyle\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{V}(R_{v}) =tr​(ZV⊤​US⊤​CS​US​ZV​EV)\displaystyle=\mathrm{tr}\!\left(Z_{V}^{\top}U_{S}^{\top}C_{S}U_{S}Z_{V}E_{V}\right)
=tr​(ZV⊤​ΛS​ZV​EV).\displaystyle=\mathrm{tr}\!\left(Z_{V}^{\top}\Lambda_{S}Z_{V}E_{V}\right).

Writing ZV=(ζi​j)Z_{V}=(\zeta_{ij}), we obtain

ℒ~V​(Rv)=∑i=1d∑j=1dηi​νj​ζj​i2.\widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{V}(R_{v})=\sum_{i=1}^{d}\sum_{j=1}^{d}\eta_{i}\nu_{j}\zeta_{ji}^{2}.

As before, (ζj​i2)(\zeta_{ji}^{2}) is doubly stochastic. The minimum is therefore attained at a permutation matrix. Since

ν1≥⋯≥νdandη1≤⋯≤ηd,\nu_{1}\geq\cdots\geq\nu_{d}\qquad\text{and}\qquad\eta_{1}\leq\cdots\leq\eta_{d},

the rearrangement inequality shows that the identity permutation is optimal. Thus one minimizer is

ZV=Id,Z_{V}=I_{d},

and therefore

Rv=US.R_{v}=U_{S}.

This proves both statements.

A.7 Justification of Surrogate Objectives in Theorem. 1

To justify the surrogate objectives in Theorem 1, we start from the downstream attention errors induced by quantizing keys and values. For the key branch, the empirical logit distortion on the calibration set is

ℓK​(Rk)=∑i=1N∑j=1i[qi​(kj−k^j)⊤]2,\ell_{K}(R_{k})=\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{i}\bigl[q_{i}(k_{j}-\hat{k}_{j})^{\top}\bigr]^{2},

where k^j=𝒬​(kj​Rk)​Rk⊤\hat{k}_{j}=\mathcal{Q}(k_{j}R_{k})R_{k}^{\top}. Writing the rotated quantization residual as

ej(K)​(Rk):=𝒬​(kj​Rk)−kj​Rk,e_{j}^{(K)}(R_{k}):=\mathcal{Q}(k_{j}R_{k})-k_{j}R_{k},

we have k^j−kj=ej(K)​(Rk)​Rk⊤\hat{k}_{j}-k_{j}=e_{j}^{(K)}(R_{k})R_{k}^{\top}, so that

ℓK​(Rk)=∑j=1Nej(K)​(Rk)​Rk⊤​Cj​Rk​ej(K)​(Rk)⊤,Cj:=∑i=1jqi⊤​qi.\ell_{K}(R_{k})=\sum_{j=1}^{N}e_{j}^{(K)}(R_{k})\,R_{k}^{\top}C_{j}R_{k}\,e_{j}^{(K)}(R_{k})^{\top},\qquad C_{j}:=\sum_{i=1}^{j}q_{i}^{\top}q_{i}.

To obtain a tractable objective, we approximate the position-dependent matrices CjC_{j} by the global query target covariance

CQ=1N​∑i=1Nqi⊤​qi.C_{Q}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}q_{i}^{\top}q_{i}.

Defining the frozen residual covariance (where RkR_{k} is fixed)

EK:=∑j=1Nej(K)​(Rk)⊤​ej(K)​(Rk),E_{K}:=\sum_{j=1}^{N}e_{j}^{(K)}(R_{k})^{\top}e_{j}^{(K)}(R_{k}),

the key-side frozen-error objective becomes, up to the constant normalization factor 1N\frac{1}{N},

ℒ~K​(Rk)=tr​(Rk⊤​CQ​Rk​EK).\tilde{\mathcal{L}}_{K}(R_{k})=\mathrm{tr}(R_{k}^{\top}C_{Q}R_{k}E_{K}).

Under a similar approximation, the value-side downstream error is heuristically, imitating that of key targets, represented by the frozen-error surrogate

ℒ~V​(Rv)=tr​(Rv⊤​CS​Rv​EV).\tilde{\mathcal{L}}_{V}(R_{v})=\mathrm{tr}\!\bigl(R_{v}^{\top}C_{S}R_{v}E_{V}\bigr).

Note that the only difference between key and value targets is whether it is purely weighted by target covariance (Q⊤​QQ^{\top}Q for keys) or it is target covariance aware (V⊤​S⊤​S​VV^{\top}S^{\top}SV). Therefore, it is reasonable that we use the same surrogate.

Appendix B Algorithm Flow and Serving Procedure

Algorithm 1 gives the concrete OSCAR procedure used in our experiments and SGLang implementation.

Appendix C Related Work

KV-cache compression. The KV cache has become a major target for post-training compression because its memory footprint grows with both context length and batch size. One line of work reduces or reprioritizes cached content through eviction, budgeted retention, attention-sink preservation, or saliency-aware precision assignment, as in H2O, SnapKV, PyramidKV, StreamingLLM, and ZipCache [1, 38, 39, 40, 41]. Other systems compress the cache through low-rank structure or cross-layer sharing, including GEAR, PALU, xKV, and MatryoshkaKV [42, 43, 44, 45]. Another line keeps all tokens but lowers the precision of the cached tensors, which is the setting most closely related to OSCAR. KIVI, KVQuant, and WKVQuant study low-bit KV storage and identify channel-wise outliers, especially in keys, as a central obstacle to aggressive quantization [22, 4, 5]. These methods show that KV quantization can be accurate, but they also leave open how far precision can be reduced without changing the serving layout.

2-bit KV quantization. Pushing KV cache quantization to 2 bits usually requires additional structure beyond naive rounding. SKVQ preserves a recent sliding window in higher precision and combines channel reordering with clipped dynamic quantization [46]. Kitty promotes sensitive key-cache channels to higher precision and combines this with a residual cache [6], while PM-KVQ uses progressive mixed precision and block-wise memory allocation for long-CoT models [47]. Related mixed-precision analyses also argue that keys should generally receive more precision than values under a fixed memory budget [48]. RotateKV uses outlier-aware adaptive rotations for 2-bit KV quantization [7]. These methods improve accuracy, but their use of channel-wise precision, residual buffers, progressive bit allocation, or adaptive layouts can complicate integration with paged KV-cache serving and fused decode kernels. OSCAR instead targets the same INT2 regime with fixed token-wise transforms and a uniform paged layout, so the quantization path can be fused into production serving.

Rotation-based quantization. Orthogonal transforms are an attractive way to make low-bit quantization more accurate without changing tensor shapes. QuaRot, HALO, and HOT show that rotations can reduce outlier concentration, redistribute activation energy, and improve low-bit rounding behavior [9, 49, 50, 51, 12]. In the KV-cache setting, TurboQuant develops a data-oblivious online vector-quantization method based on random rotations, per-coordinate Lloyd–Max quantization, and QJL-style residual coding [36]. KVLinC combines Hadamard rotation with lightweight linear correction to reduce attention errors under aggressive KV quantization [52]. OSCAR follows the same broad rotation principle, but asks which rotation target is appropriate for INT2 KV cache quantization. While [11] effectively mitigates outliers at 4-bit using learned rotations, it collapses at extreme INT2 levels because its rotations fail to align with the actual covariance structures consumed by downstream attention. Furthermore, it focuses primarily on theoretical algorithmic gains, lacking the custom kernels and system-level integration necessary for real-world deployment in modern serving frameworks.

Covariance-aware calibration. A related family uses calibration data or covariance information to choose quantization or compression directions. For weight quantization, GPTQ and QuIP use Hessian information to guide low-bit rounding [24, 20]; for low-rank compression, Drone, ASVD, SVD-LLM, SVD-LLMv2, and CorDA use calibration activations or data-aware statistics to guide factorization [53, 54, 55, 56, 57]. On the KV-cache side, HaPPI, CARE, RecalKV, and CommonKV apply covariance- or Hessian-aware factorization to keys and values for KV-side projection or compression [58, 59, 60, 61]. Post-training quantization methods such as SmoothQuant, AWQ, GPTQ, and OmniQuant also use calibration data to estimate scales, equivalent transformations, clipping thresholds, or second-order surrogates before deployment [23, 62, 24, 63]. OSCAR specializes this calibration paradigm to attention: it estimates targets covariance induced by downstream attention, rather than raw-cache statistics, and uses them to derive fixed key/value rotations and clip thresholds for serving-time INT2 KV quantization.

Appendix D Discussion

Limitations.

Our theoretical analysis explains why the proposed covariance-target rotations are optimal under a frozen-error surrogate with explicit assumptions (Appendix A), but it is not yet a full proof of optimality for the complete autoregressive decoding process. Tightening this gap—for example, by proving when the calibration estimator is unbiased or optimal for the true attention objective—is an important direction for future work. We also focus on INT2 KV-cache quantization and do not explore other numerical formats or mixed-format designs, such as NVFP4, different weight precisions, or weight/KV precision combinations, which may create different accuracy–systems trade-offs. Similarly, OSCAR uses a fixed bit-width for quantized history tokens and does not study per-token bit allocation or thought-adaptive token precision, which recent work such as ThinKV suggests may be useful for long reasoning traces [64]. On the systems side, our implementation targets SGLang-style paged serving on H100 GPUs; additional tuning is needed to understand the best kernel design on other hardware such as B200. Finally, OSCAR is orthogonal to channel-wise or mixed-precision methods: applying attention-aware/Hadamard rotations inside channel-wise schemes may further improve accuracy, but combining these ideas while preserving an efficient serving layout remains open.

Broader impact.

OSCAR reduces KV-cache memory and serving cost, which can make long-context LLM inference more accessible and energy-efficient. At the same time, cheaper long-context inference may also lower the cost of deploying powerful generative models, including applications with misuse risks; OSCAR does not introduce new model capabilities, but should be deployed with the same safety and usage controls as the underlying LLMs.

Appendix E Additional Experimental Results

E.1 Additional Motivation Figure

The additional motivation figures are shown in Fig 9 and Fig 10.

E.2 Full Table For Main Accuracy Run

Table 11 reports the five independent runs for the Qwen3-4B-Thinking main OSCAR result. Table 12–14 report the five independent runs used to summarize Qwen3-8B OSCAR accuracy under three INT2 grouping configurations. Table 15–17 report the Qwen3-32B OSCAR runs. Table 18–20 report the GLM-4.7-FP8 detailed runs for Naive INT2, QuaRot/Hadamard rotation, and OSCAR.

E.3 Ablation Detail Runs

Table 21 reports the individual runs behind the rotation-decomposition study in Table 4.

Table 22 reports the individual runs behind the sink/recent-window study in Table 5.

Table 23 reports the per-task results behind the clip-threshold grid in Table 6.

Table 24 reports the individual runs behind the calibration-data regime study in Table 7.

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.
